title: 1 Kings 2
subtitle: David's Charge; Solomon Secures the Throne

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — David's Last Words: Be Strong and Keep the Law

heb.gloss: be strong and be a man
heb.paragraph: David's charge to Solomon (v.2) echoes the language of Moses to Joshua (Deut 31:7; Josh 1:6–9). The phraseḥăzaq(be strong) followed by the covenant condition ("keep the charge of the LORD") establishes Solomon's reign on the same foundation as the conquest: courage grounded in Torah obedience.

heb.gloss: the way of all the earth
heb.paragraph: David says he is going "the way of all the earth" (v.2) — a dignified euphemism for death found also in Josh 23:14. The phrase universalises death: even the greatest king walks the same road as every commoner. No human dynasty endures in itself; only God's covenant endures.

refs.note: The "Law of the King" — the king must write a copy of the Torah, read it daily, and not consider himself above his brothers. David's charge to Solomon fulfils this Deuteronomic requirement.
"Be strong and very courageous. Be careful to obey all the law my servant Moses gave you." Moses to Joshua; now David to Solomon — the same charge transmitted across three generations of leadership.
Joab's murder of Abner: "Joab took him aside in the gateway... and stabbed him in the stomach." David could not punish Joab then; he entrusts the reckoning to Solomon.

notes.note: Calvin: David does not say "Be a great warrior" or "Expand the borders" but "Keep the charge of the LORD your God." The first duty of the ruler is personal godliness. If the king walks with God, the kingdom prospers; if he departs, the kingdom crumbles. This is the summary of all that follows in Kings.

Calvin: The death of the saints is described as sleep — they rest with their ancestors, awaiting the resurrection. David's death is peaceful because his conscience is at rest, his succession is secured, and his God is faithful. Let every believer prepare for death with the same confidence.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note:ḥăzaq wĕhāyîtā lĕʾîšliterally "be strong and become a man" — the imperative + weqatal sequence expresses consequence: by being strong in Torah, Solomon becomes the kind of man who can rule. Manhood is not a given but an achievement of obedience.

NET Note: The conditional clauseʾim-yišmĕrû bānêkā ʾet-darkām(if your sons keep their way) introduces the conditionality that structures the entire Deuteronomistic evaluation of every king who follows. Each king is measured against this single criterion.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wiseman: The accumulation of legal terms in v.3 —mišmeret(charge),ḥuqqôt(statutes),mišpāṭîm(judgments),ʿēdôt(testimonies) — mirrors the comprehensive vocabulary of Deuteronomy itself. The king's first duty is to be a Torah scholar, not a war leader.

Wiseman: David's 40-year reign (7 in Hebron, 33 in Jerusalem) aligns with the conventional ANE rounding for a full generation of rule. The Assyrian King List and Babylonian chronicles use similar formulae.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Provan: David's deathbed speech is the Deuteronomistic narrator's theological programme in miniature. The conditional covenant, the Torah as the basis of kingship, and the promise of dynastic continuity — all three pillars of the narrator's theology are stated in four verses. Everything that follows in 1–2 Kings is commentary on this charge.

Provan: The political instructions (vv.5–9) create moral complexity for the reader. Is David being vindictive or just? The narrator does not resolve the tension. Solomon must exercise wisdom — the very gift he will soon request — to navigate between mercy and justice.

hist.context: David's deathbed charge has two layers: the theological (vv.2–4) and the political (vv.5–9). The theological charge is pure Deuteronomy: keep God's commands, walk in his ways, observe his decrees — "so that you may prosper in all you do" (v.3). This is the Deuteronomistic thesis applied to kingship. The political charge is harder: deal with Joab (who murdered Abner and Amasa) and Shimei (who cursed the LORD's anointed). David could not act against them in his lifetime due to political constraints; he entrusts justice to Solomon. The dying king gives his son both a torah and a hit list — and the narrator presents both as legitimate charges.

notes.note: The narrative of Kings presents Israel's history as a theological argument: obedience to God's covenant brings blessing; disobedience brings judgment. This section continues that theme, demonstrating that God's sovereignty operates through human choices and their consequences. Every passage in Kings ultimately points to the need for a perfect King who will fulfill what no earthly monarch could.

The theological framework of Kings evaluates every monarch by a single standard: fidelity to the covenant expressed in exclusive worship of Yahweh at the chosen place. Political success, military prowess, and economic prosperity are irrelevant apart from covenant obedience. This standard anticipates the NT principle that faithfulness to God's Word is the only measure of true success (Rev 2:10).

The practical lesson for every believer is that obedience to God's Word is the only path to genuine blessing. The kings who prospered spiritually were those who feared the Lord and walked in His ways. The same principle governs the Christian life: 'If you love Me, keep My commandments' (John 14:15). Obedience is not the means of salvation but the evidence of it.

sections.header: Verses 13–46 — Adonijah, Abiathar, Joab, Shimei: Four Threats Removed

heb.gloss: the kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon
heb.paragraph: The chapter's concluding formula (v.46) —wĕhammamlākāh nāḵōnāh bĕyad-Šĕlōmōh— uses the verbkûn(niphal: to be established, firm). This is the same root God used in the Davidic covenant: "I willestablishhis throne" (2 Sam 7:13). The narrator signals that God's promise is now fulfilled in Solomon.
Solomon tells the sons of Barzillai to show chesed (v.7), not Shimei. Shimei receives house arrest and conditions (vv.36-46).

refs.note: "I will raise up for myself a faithful priest." The exile of Abiathar (v.27) fulfils the prophecy against Eli's house spoken 100+ years earlier. The narrator explicitly notes this (v.27).
Shimei's original cursing of David at Bahurim: "Get out, get out, you man of blood!" David spared him then; Solomon gives him a second chance with a clear condition.
Joab's two murders — Abner killed in the gate of Hebron, Amasa stabbed while greeting him. Both were acts of treachery against men at peace. David's dying charge finally brings justice.

notes.note: Calvin: God's Word spoken against Eli's house a hundred years prior finds exact fulfilment in Abiathar's exile. Let no one imagine that the passage of time weakens divine prophecy. God's memory is longer than ours, and his justice arrives on his schedule, not ours.

Calvin: The establishment of Solomon's kingdom teaches that God uses secondary means — even hard political decisions — to accomplish his purposes. Solomon's wisdom is not separate from his decisiveness. The godly ruler must sometimes act firmly to secure the peace that enables worship.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The narrator's aside — "So Solomon removed Abiathar... fulfilling the word the LORD had spoken at Shiloh about the house of Eli" — is a characteristic Deuteronomistic editorial intervention, connecting the present event to a prior prophetic word and demonstrating the reliability of divine speech.

NET Note:wĕhammamlākāh nāḵōnāh bĕyad-Šĕlōmōh— the niphal participlenāḵōnāh(established, secure) echoes the covenant promise of 2 Sam 7:16 (wĕnāḵôn). The narrator uses the same vocabulary to signal covenant fulfilment.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Wiseman: Abiathar's exile to Anathoth (his ancestral estate, located about 3 miles northeast of Jerusalem) is significant archaeologically. Anathoth is identified with modern Ras el-Kharrubeh near 'Anata. It would later become the hometown of Jeremiah (Jer 1:1) — a descendant of the very priestly line Solomon exiled.

Wiseman: Shimei's slaves fled to Achish king of Gath — the same Philistine city where David had taken refuge (1 Sam 27). The Gath connection underscores the ongoing political relationships between Israelite and Philistine territories even in the early Solomonic period.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Provan: The Abishag request is the narrator's first test of Solomon's wisdom. Before the dream at Gibeon (ch. 3), Solomon already demonstrates the discernment he will formally receive. The narrative sequence implies that wisdom is a gift given to those who are already inclined toward it.

Provan: The fourfold consolidation creates a clean break between the Davidic era of civil war and intrigue and the Solomonic era of peace and construction. The narrator clears the stage so that the temple can be built in a kingdom at rest — the precondition David himself articulated (2 Sam 7:1).

hist.context: Solomon's consolidation of power proceeds through four episodes, each a test of wisdom. Adonijah requests Abishag (David's last companion) — a thinly veiled claim to the throne, since possessing a king's concubine implied royal succession (cf. 2 Sam 16:21–22). Solomon sees through it instantly. Abiathar is exiled (fulfilling the prophecy against Eli's house, 1 Sam 2:27–36). Joab flees to the altar but is executed there — unlike Adonijah in ch. 1, Joab's crimes are capital. Shimei violates his oath and is executed. Each episode demonstrates Solomon's discernment: the man who will soon ask God for wisdom is already exercising it.

notes.note: The narrative of Kings presents Israel's history as a theological argument: obedience to God's covenant brings blessing; disobedience brings judgment. This section continues that theme, demonstrating that God's sovereignty operates through human choices and their consequences. Every passage in Kings ultimately points to the need for a perfect King who will fulfill what no earthly monarch could.

The theological framework of Kings evaluates every monarch by a single standard: fidelity to the covenant expressed in exclusive worship of Yahweh at the chosen place. Political success, military prowess, and economic prosperity are irrelevant apart from covenant obedience. This standard anticipates the NT principle that faithfulness to God's Word is the only measure of true success (Rev 2:10).

The practical lesson for every believer is that obedience to God's Word is the only path to genuine blessing. The kings who prospered spiritually were those who feared the Lord and walked in His ways. The same principle governs the Christian life: 'If you love Me, keep My commandments' (John 14:15). Obedience is not the means of salvation but the evidence of it.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV\u201cNow you yourself know what Joab son of Zeruiah did to me\u2014what he did to the two commanders of Israel\u2019s armies, Abner son of Ner and Amasa son of Jether. He killed them, shedding their blood in peacetime as if in battle, and with that blood he stained the belt around his waist and the sandals on his feet.ESV\u201cMoreover, you also know what Joab the son of Zeruiah did to me, how he dealt with the two commanders of the armies of Israel, Abner the son of Ner, and Amasa the son of Jether, whom he killed, avenging in time of peace for blood that had been shed in war, and putting the blood of war on the belt around his waist and on the sandals on his feet.NIVand that the Lord may keep his promise to me: \u2018If your descendants watch how they live, and if they walk faithfully before me with all their heart and soul, you will never fail to have a successor on the throne of Israel.\u2019ESVthat the LORD may establish his word that he spoke concerning me, saying, \u2018If your sons pay close attention to their way, to walk before me in faithfulness with all their heart and with all their soul, you shall not lack a man on the throne of Israel.\u2019

hebtext.gloss: be strong and be a man
hebtext.note: David's charge to Solomon (v.2) echoes the language of Moses to Joshua (Deut 31:7; Josh 1:6–9). The phraseḥăzaq(be strong) followed by the covenant condition ("keep the charge of the LORD") establishes Solomon's reign on the same foundation as the conquest: courage grounded in Torah obedience.

hebtext.gloss: the way of all the earth
hebtext.note: David says he is going "the way of all the earth" (v.2) — a dignified euphemism for death found also in Josh 23:14. The phrase universalises death: even the greatest king walks the same road as every commoner. No human dynasty endures in itself; only God's covenant endures.

hebtext.gloss: the kingdom was established in the hand of Solomon
hebtext.note: The chapter's concluding formula (v.46) —wĕhammamlākāh nāḵōnāh bĕyad-Šĕlōmōh— uses the verbkûn(niphal: to be established, firm). This is the same root God used in the Davidic covenant: "I willestablishhis throne" (2 Sam 7:13). The narrator signals that God's promise is now fulfilled in Solomon.
David tells Solomon to show chesed to the sons of Barzillai (v.7), referencing covenant loyalty. The Shimei narrative (vv.36-46) involves confinement to Jerusalem, not chesed.

themes.note: David's Charge; Solomon Secures the Throne

coaching.tip: David's deathbed instructions mix spiritual counsel ('walk in obedience') with political hits — settle scores with Joab and Shimei. The blend is unsettling. David's final words reveal a man who is simultaneously a man of faith and a calculating power broker. The Bible gives you both.
